_comment: Module-board narratives — long-form audit prose per board num. Source: operator-approved HS narrative companion (2026-06-11). Rendered below the structured charter in boardCharter(); keyed by ModuleBoard.num.

narratives.1.01:
A protected reporting channel sits at the foundation of the firm's internal-controls framework. The Grievances module manages whistleblowing and grievance reports, whether submitted anonymously or under name, and carries each through triage, investigation and remediation to a recorded close. Reporter protection is enforced from the moment a report is lodged: identity is shielded, and retaliation is treated as a control failure in its own right. The Compliance Officer owns the queue and works every matter to an outcome; where a report discloses a deliberate reporting failure, escalation to the Managing Director occurs the same day, without exception.

These arrangements give effect to the internal controls and whistleblowing obligations of Cabinet 10/2019 Art.21 and FDL 10/2025 Art.20. Reports are triaged within the service-level standard set by the compliance programme, and the module operates to the control cadence applicable to each matter rather than to a fixed calendar. In practice a submission enters the board as a new report and passes through triage and investigation; matters warranting senior attention move to the escalated stage for the MLRO or Managing Director, and nothing leaves the board until remediation is complete and the outcome recorded.

Evidence is the discipline that makes the channel credible to an inspector. Every action taken in the module, from first receipt to closure, is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain maintained under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, so the sequence of decisions cannot be rewritten after the fact. A daily attestation posts to the module's board at 06:00 UTC, confirming the control operated. Records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis: closed matters leave the working queue but remain permanently recoverable, and a reporter's protection does not lapse merely because the report has been resolved.
narratives.1.02:
Customer due diligence does not end when a file is approved. Cabinet 10/2019 Art.7 and FATF R.10 require that CDD remain ongoing and that records be kept current, and the pKYC Monitor is the firm's standing answer to that duty: perpetual KYC, in which every customer's CDD lifecycle is monitored continuously and refresh is driven by events rather than by the calendar alone. A material change - a new ownership interest, an altered risk indicator, adverse information - triggers a CDD refresh at once, without waiting for the next periodic cycle to fall due. Overdue refreshes are flagged on the board and stay visible until acted upon. The Compliance Officer owns the module and works the queue as triggers arise, with the periodic review regime continuing to operate underneath as a floor.

Operationally, a triggered customer arrives on the board as unreviewed, moves into review while the refreshed information is obtained and assessed, and is escalated as pending decision where the change alters the risk picture; the file completes once the record is current again. Each of these movements, and the trigger that caused it, is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain kept under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, so an inspector can reconstruct not only what was refreshed but why and when.

The module attests daily at 06:00 UTC on its own board, evidencing that the monitoring loop ran. Records are held for five years and are archived rather than deleted, which keeps a customer's fully refresh history available across the life of the relationship and beyond.

The cadence is the control cadence applicable to each customer and event class, and that is the point of the design: perpetual KYC adapts its tempo to the risk in front of it.
narratives.1.03:
Every new customer enters the firm through one controlled door. The Onboarding Wizard is a guided intake that walks the handling officer through each step of new-customer acceptance and routes the file onward to CDD, to screening and, where required, to the MLRO. Its design principle is the absence of gaps. The intake path cannot be completed while required CDD fields remain empty: the wizard withholds completion until identification data, ownership detail and risk-relevant particulars are all in place. There is no side entrance, and no manual shortcut that bypasses the sequence.

The module runs per new customer, which is its natural cadence; it is exercised as often as the firm takes on business and lies dormant otherwise. The Compliance Officer owns the intake and is accountable for every file it produces. On the board, a prospective customer arrives as unreviewed, progresses into review as the wizard steps are worked, and where a judgement is needed sits at escalated pending decision rather than drifting; the file completes only when the full record exists.

Two further disciplines apply, and both matter at inspection. First, every action in the wizard is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, so the construction of each customer file can be replayed field by field, and a daily attestation posts to the module's board at 06:00 UTC confirming the control ran. Second, intake records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis.

The legal anchor for the whole arrangement is controlled CDD intake under Cabinet 10/2019 Art.4 and Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.10: due diligence begins before the relationship does, and this module is where the firm makes that requirement real.
narratives.1.04:
Prospective clients reach the firm first through the Client Portal, a self-service onboarding channel through which they submit their legal identity, beneficial owners and directors. What they cannot do is activate themselves. Each submission is screened automatically on receipt, and the relationship remains inert until the MLRO has recorded a disposition. The gate is fail-closed by design: identification, verification and screening are enforced as hard preconditions, and where any of them is incomplete, or the MLRO has not decided, the answer is no activation rather than provisional access.

The obligation served is risk-based CDD before a business relationship, anchored in Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025, Cabinet 10/2019 Art.4-8 and FATF R.10. The control operates for every new customer, at onboarding, before activation. There is no volume threshold beneath which it relaxes.

Ownership runs from the Compliance Officer, who manages the intake and the screening output, to the MLRO, who alone disposes. On the board a submission lands as unreviewed, is taken into review while verification and screening complete, and sits at escalated pending decision until the MLRO rules; only then does the file complete.

The evidential record is deliberately deeper here than elsewhere. Every action - each screening pass, each verification step, the MLRO's disposition itself - is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module attests daily at 06:00 UTC on its board. Onboarding records are retained for five years and the decision log for ten, both on an archive-never-delete footing, so the basis on which any client was admitted remains demonstrable for the full supervisory horizon, including the identity of the deciding MLRO and the screening position at the moment of decision.
narratives.1.05:
Behind every corporate customer stands a natural person, and Cabinet 58/2020, Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.19 and the MOE UBO filing regime oblige the firm to know who that person is. The UBO Declaration module captures and risk-rates every natural-person beneficial owner holding twenty-five per cent or more, and it is built for the hard cases: layered holdings, nominee arrangements and structures assembled to put distance between ownership and name. Where the true beneficial owner cannot be established, activation is blocked. That is the control in a single sentence, and it admits no exception. Bearer-share features or nominee indicators do not merely raise a note; they trigger enhanced due diligence as of course.

The module operates at onboarding and again on any ownership change, so the declaration is a living record rather than a snapshot taken once. The Compliance Officer owns the register day to day, with the MLRO assuming ownership whenever EDD is triggered. A declaration arrives on the board unreviewed, is worked in review while ownership evidence is assembled, and is escalated pending decision where the structure resists resolution; the file completes only when the chain of ownership stands established.

For inspection purposes the module's value lies in its trail. Every capture, rating and decision is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the board carries a daily 06:00 UTC attestation confirming the control ran. Declarations are retained for five years, archived and never deleted, which means the ownership picture the firm relied upon at any point in a relationship can be reproduced exactly as it stood, together with the enhanced due diligence it provoked.
narratives.1.06:
Politically exposed persons are accepted by decision, never by default. The PEP Profiles module establishes, for each identified PEP, the tier of exposure, the source of wealth and source of funds, the surrounding relationship network and the enhanced due diligence measures that will govern the relationship. None of this is advisory. Senior-management approval is required before a PEP is on boarded and before an existing relationship with a person later identified as a PEP is continued, and the EDD measures recorded in the profile are mandatory in application, not a menu. Ownership sits jointly with the MLRO and senior management, which mirrors the obligation itself: Cabinet 10/2019 Art.15 and FATF R.12 require PEP identification, senior approval and enhanced monitoring as a package, and the module administers the three together.

Profiles are created at onboarding and re-assessed at each periodic review, so tiering and EDD measures track the customer's actual circumstances rather than their position on the day of intake. On the board a profile enters unreviewed, moves through review as wealth and funds evidence is gathered, and is escalated pending decision for the senior approval the law demands; only a recorded approval completes it. Every step is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, with the result that the firm can show an inspector not merely that a PEP was approved but who approved them, on what evidence and when. The module's board carries a daily attestation at 06:00 UTC. Profile records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis, preserving the approval history across successive re-assessments and any change of tier.
narratives.1.07:
Responsible sourcing expectations now reach well beyond sanctions lists, and the ESG Risk module is where the firm meets them. Drawing its standards from the OECD DDG, LBMA RGG v9 and the CSDDD-aligned supply-chain duty, it scores environmental, social and governance risk for each counterparty, overlays a machine-learning risk view on the underlying indicators, and maps the regulatory exposure each relationship carries. The output is concrete: an ESG score recorded against every assessed entity, not a narrative impression.

The control bites at the top of the scale. High ESG-risk outcomes are routed into enhanced due diligence review rather than left standing as data points, so a poor score carries a procedural consequence. The Compliance Officer owns the module and works assessments to the control cadence applicable to each counterparty class. On the board, an assessment arrives unreviewed, is worked in review while scoring completes, and is escalated pending decision where the EDD route is engaged; closure follows the recorded outcome.

As elsewhere on the platform, the evidence regime is uniform. Each scoring run, overlay result and routing decision is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module posts its attestation to its board daily at 06:00 UTC. Records are retained for five years and are archived, never deleted. An inspector can therefore trace any counterparty's ESG history from first score to latest review, including each occasion on which a high-risk outcome was escalated into EDD, and test that the scoring discipline operates as described rather than as aspiration. The firm treats that traceability as part of the control itself, since a score that cannot be evidenced would persuade no supervisor and protect no supply chain.
narratives.1.08:
Suppliers and other third parties are subjected to the same diligence discipline as customers, adjusted for the different risk they present. The Supplier DD module conducts third-party and supplier due diligence with an AI risk assessment over each file and then holds every supplier to a re-assessment schedule fixed by tier: critical suppliers are re-assessed annually, significant suppliers every eighteen months, and standard suppliers every twenty-four months. The tiering is the control. No supplier ages out of scrutiny, and the greater the dependency or exposure, the shorter the leash. The Compliance Officer owns the register, assigns tiers and is accountable for the cadence being met in practice rather than on paper.

A supplier file enters the board unreviewed, passes into review while due diligence is performed, and is escalated pending decision where findings call for judgement beyond the standard path; completed files return at their tier-determined date and the cycle begins again. Every assessment, tier assignment and re-assessment is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board attests daily at 06:00 UTC that the control is live. Records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis, so the full re-assessment history of any supplier remains examinable end to end. The legal footing is counterparty and third-party risk management under FATF R.10, the OECD DDG and MOE DPMS supply-chain expectations, which together require precisely what the module delivers: knowledge of who the firm depends upon, refreshed at a frequency proportionate to the risk of depending upon them.

A supplier relationship the firm cannot provide evidence is, for these purposes, a relationship it does not understand.
narratives.1.09:
Periodic re-KYC is where customer files are forced back onto the desk. The CDD Review module runs the firm's risk-tiered review programme: high-risk relationships are re-reviewed every three months, medium-risk every six months, and low-risk annually. An AI adequacy check reads each file for completeness ahead of the human review, surfacing gaps before the reviewer opens the record rather than after.

The cadence is enforced, not advisory. Overdue files are flagged and remain flagged until reviewed; the module does not permit a quiet lapse. This is the practical machinery behind Cabinet 10/2019 Art.7 and FATF R.10, which require that CDD be ongoing and that records be kept current for as long as the relationship subsists.

The Compliance Officer owns the programme and conducts the reviews, escalating to the MLRO where a refreshed file changes the risk picture or resists completion. On the board, files move from pending documents into under review, and each relationship cycles back through the periodic re-KYC stage at its tier-determined date. Approval and rejection are both genuine outcomes; rejection is used where the refreshed position can no longer support the relationship continuing on its existing terms.

Each review, adequacy check and disposition is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module attests on its board daily at 06:00 UTC. Review records are retained for five years and archived rather than deleted, which lets an inspector test the cadence claim directly: select any customer, and the intervals between that customer's reviews are sitting in the chain, in order, with the adequacy findings attached. The firm regards a review it cannot evidence as a review that did not happen.
narratives.1.10:
Opaque corporate structures are mapped here, not merely noted. The Ownership Explorer takes each customer's declared ownership and builds the full chain from legal entity to ultimate control, detecting along the way the signatures of concealment: shell-company indicators and layering across jurisdictions chosen for distance rather than substance. The operating rule is look-through. Analysis does not stop at the first holding company; it continues until ultimate control is established, or until the structure is shown to resist establishment, which is itself a finding with consequences.

Two structural features trigger mandatory escalation: chains running deeper than four layers, and circular structures in which entities hold one another. Either routes the file to EDD or legal review. The Compliance Officer owns the module, with the MLRO taking complex structures personally. Mapping occurs at onboarding and again on any structure change, so the picture on file tracks the structure as it actually stands rather than as it stood at intake. On the board, a structure enters unreviewed, is worked in review as the chain is traced, and sits at escalated pending decision where depth or circularity demands a ruling.

Every mapping run, trigger and review outcome is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, a daily attestation posts to the module's board at 06:00 UTC, and records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted. The obligation discharged is the understanding of ownership and control required by Cabinet 58/2020 and FATF R.24/25. Our position to an inspector is short. For any corporate customer, we can produce the chain we traced, the date we traced it, and the decision each anomaly received.
narratives.1.11:
The firm's defenses are staffed by people, and the Employees module keeps the record proving those people are fit to staff them. It maintains the staff register: each employee's Emirates ID and passport details with their expiry dates, alongside the currency of their AML/CFT training. The watching is automatic. Documents approaching expiry are flagged thirty days in advance, lapsed documents and overdue training are flagged without grace, and the register is monitored continuously rather than reviewed in batches. Nothing depends on an employee remembering to volunteer that a document has aged out. Ownership is shared between the Compliance Officer and HR, which mirrors the subject matter: an employment record with a compliance edge.

Fit-and-proper staffing and employee screening are required by Cabinet 10/2019 Art.21 and Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.16, and the register is the firm's running evidence of both. A new joiner sits at documents pending until the identity and training record is complete; thereafter the file rests at compliant unless the expiring-soon stage pulls it back into attention, and departure closes the file. Short, mechanical and difficult to evade. Every change to the register, every flag raised and every flag cleared is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module attests on its board daily at 06:00 UTC. Records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis, so the firm can demonstrate, for any past date, exactly which staff held current identification and current training, which had been flagged, and what was done about the flag.
narratives.1.12:
Cabinet 10/2019 Art.21, Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.16 and the MOE DNFBP guidance each place a training duty on the firm, and the Training module is the instrument by which that duty is administered and evidenced. It runs the annual AML/CFT programme: assignment of training to staff, tracking of completion, monitoring of expiry, and the recertification cycle that brings every employee back through the material at least annually.

Completion is tracked per person, and lapses are flagged rather than absorbed. An employee whose certification has aged out remains visible on the board until the position is corrected, and the Compliance Officer, who owns the programme, is accountable for chasing each flag to closure. The board's stages tell the story plainly: training is assigned, moves through in progress, and in due course surfaces again at recertification due. There is no terminal state in which a member of staff is finished with training for good. The cycle is the point.

Evidence of training is among the first things an inspector requests, and the module is built to answer without preparation. Every assignment, completion and lapse flag is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, the module's board carries a daily attestation at 06:00 UTC, and records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis.

The firm can therefore produce, for any employee and any date, what training had been assigned, what had been completed, and whether anything stood overdue, with the answer drawn from a chain that cannot be edited after the fact rather than from a spreadsheet assembled for the occasion.
narratives.1.13:
No onboarding decision stands on one signature. The Approvals module tracks each onboarding approval - its status, the customer's risk score and the approved destinations - and subjects every one of them to four-eyes control. Dual authorization is required without exception: the Compliance Officer acts as maker, the MLRO as checker, and both signatories are recorded against the decision. A decision bearing one name is not a decision; it is a proposal awaiting its reviewer.

The discipline gives effect to the internal controls and segregation of duties required by Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.20 and Cabinet 10/2019 Art.21, and it operates per onboarding decision, which is to say at exactly the frequency the business generates decisions to control. On the board a proposal waits at awaiting reviewer until the checker takes it up, then resolves to approved or is returned for revision; a return travels back to the maker with the reviewer's reasons, and the cycle repeats until the file can honestly carry two names. Both signatures, every status movement and every return are written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, so the separation between maker and checker is provable rather than asserted.

The module attests daily on its board at 06:00 UTC. Approval records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted, and that record answers the inspection question that matters most here: not whether the firm claims segregation of duties, but whether any onboarding decision can be found that escaped it. None can, because this tracker is the only route an approval may lawfully take through the firm.

RISK & AML OPS
narratives.2.01:
All of the firm's risk-based choices trace back to one assessment. Cabinet 10/2019 Art.2-3 and FATF R.1 require a documented business-wide ML/TF risk assessment, and the EWRA / BWRA module is where that assessment is built, maintained and approved. It assembles the enterprise-wide view of money laundering and terrorist financing risk and produces an AI-drafted board report that puts the findings in a form the Board can interrogate and own. Ownership runs from the MLRO, who prepares the assessment and stands behind it, to the Board, whose approval is required before the assessment takes effect. The risk-based approach applied everywhere else on the platform is derived from this document; tiering, cadences and EDD triggers inherit their justification from it.

The assessment is refreshed at least annually and on material change, so a shift in the business or its environment reopens the work rather than waiting for an anniversary. On the board the assessment is worked in review and sits at escalated pending decision until Board approval is recorded; only then does it complete. The drafting steps, the AI board report, the MLRO's submission and the Board's approval are each written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module attests daily at 06:00 UTC on its board. Assessments are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis, which preserves the succession of versions: an inspector can read this year's EWRA against last year's and see what changed, why it changed, and who approved the change. We regard that visible lineage as part of the assessment itself, since a risk-based programme is only as defensible as the assessment it rests on.
narratives.2.02:
For a DPMS business, where the goods come from is a compliance question before it is a commercial one. The MOE/OECD DDG, LBMA RGG v9 and MD 68/2024 set the responsible-sourcing expectations for the sector, and the Supply Chain & Responsible Sourcing module is the firm's working implementation of them.

The method is the OECD five-step due diligence framework, applied per supplier and refreshed at annual review. Geographic risk and human-rights risk are assessed together, with a CAHRA exposure assessment identifying where a supply chain touches conflict-affected and high-risk areas. This is not an overlay on commercial onboarding; it is a discipline of its own, owned jointly by the Compliance Officer and the DPMS compliance function.

Findings have routes, not just ratings. A check moves into under review while the five-step analysis is worked and resolves to cleared, with a dedicated sanctions-hit stage for the occasions when screening within the supply chain surfaces a listed party. That stage exists because sourcing risk and sanctions risk meet in practice, and the board is built to record the meeting honestly.

Every check, CAHRA assessment and clearance is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module attests daily at 06:00 UTC on its board. Records are retained for five years, archived rather than deleted. The firm can therefore evidence, supplier by supplier and year by year, that the five-step framework was applied, what exposure was found and how each finding was resolved, which is the substance of what a Ministry inspection of responsible sourcing will ask. The answer is assembled continuously, in the ordinary course of the work, and not retrospectively for the inspection itself.
narratives.2.03:
Responsible-minerals assurance for the firm's smelter and refiner base is administered through this module, which records the RMAP audit status of every smelter and refiner on which our precious-metals supply chain depends and preserves the Step-4 audit history behind each conformance determination. The discipline is deliberately narrow: no smelter or refiner is relied upon without a current, evidenced view of its RMAP standing, and the DPMS compliance function owns that view end to end. It sits within the firm's RISK & AML OPS estate alongside the wider responsible-sourcing controls.

Assessments operate per supplier and are refreshed annually. Incoming items land on the module board as unreviewed arrivals, pass into review by the DPMS compliance team and, where the audit position is unclear or adverse, are escalated pending decision before being completed and closed. Escalations carry the underlying Step-4 evidence forward so the decision-maker sees the same audit history the reviewer saw. Nothing is closed without a recorded determination, and the annual refresh is tracked per supplier so a conformance view cannot quietly go stale between reviews.

Our regulatory anchor is upstream and downstream assurance under OECD DDG Annex II, supplemented by LBMA RGG v9; together these frame the audit-reliance expectations the Ministry of Economy applies to the DPMS sector. Evidentially, every action taken in the module is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain the firm maintains under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and a daily attestation posts to the module's board at 06:00 UTC confirming the record is intact. Records are retained for five years and are archived, never deleted, so an inspector can reconstruct the conformance position of any smelter or refiner as it stood on any given day.
narratives.2.04:
Ministerial Decree 68/2024 sets the responsible-sourcing bar for the UAE gold and precious-metals sector, and this module is where the firm evidences that the bar is met. The Compliance Officer, who owns the workflow, records a five-step assessment under the OECD DDG for every supply-chain counterparty and tracks our position against the Decree and against the LBMA RGG responsible-sourcing requirements in parallel. A gap identified in any assessment does not rest with the assessor; it escalates to the MLRO for decision, and that decision is recorded before the item moves on.

Counterparty assessments arrive on the board as unreviewed items, progress into review, and conclude either directly at completion or through the escalated stage, where a pending decision is held until the MLRO rules. Closed items are filed to the board's archive rather than removed. The module operates on its assigned control cadence, and assessments are re-opened when a counterparty's circumstances change materially, so the recorded five-step position reflects the relationship as it stands today, not as it stood at onboarding.

Evidence is the point of the exercise. Every assessment, escalation and disposition is committed to the firm's append-only, hash-linked audit chain in accordance with Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries a daily attestation at 06:00 UTC confirming the chain remains unbroken. Records are held for five years on an archive-never-delete basis. When the Ministry of Economy asks how the firm implements the OECD five-step due-diligence guidance in practice, the answer is not a policy document; it is this register, counterparty by counterparty, with the escalation trail intact.
narratives.2.05:
Conformance to the OECD Due Diligence Guidance is tracked here as a discipline in its own right, separate from the counterparty-level assessments it underpins. The firm maintains a five-step conformance checklist spanning the full OECD DDG cycle, and the DPMS compliance function, which owns the module, walks the entire checklist once a year on the annual cadence.

The OECD DDG is the recognized five-step standard for DPMS supply chains, and it is the standard against which the Ministry of Economy examines the sector. For a dealer in precious metals and stones, supply-chain provenance is the core laundering exposure, so the firm keeps its conformance position current, evidenced and owned rather than asserted in a policy.

Checklist items enter the board as unreviewed arrivals and pass through review by DPMS compliance. Where a step cannot be evidenced to the reviewer's satisfaction, the item escalates and is held pending decision; only a recorded determination moves it onward to completion and closure. Short of that, it stays open. Interim changes in the supply chain can re-open individual steps between annual cycles, so the checklist follows the business rather than the calendar alone.

The evidentiary treatment matches the rest of the compliance estate. Each review, escalation and closure writes to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain maintained under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, the board posts its daily attestation at 06:00 UTC, and records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted. An inspector reading the module therefore sees not only the current conformance state but every prior annual cycle, with the reasoning behind each determination preserved. That history is what separates asserted conformance from demonstrated conformance, and the firm holds itself to the latter.
narratives.2.06:
Before the firm places supply-chain reliance on any smelter or refiner, that facility's conformance status is checked against the Responsible Minerals Initiative / RMAP list held in this module. The sequencing is the control: the lookup precedes the reliance, never the reverse. The module maintains the RMAP-conformant smelter database and serves conformance lookups to the rest of the minerals due-diligence estate, giving the Compliance Officer, who owns it, a single authoritative answer to whether a given facility carries a current independent audit.

The legal logic derives from OECD DDG Step 4, which permits a downstream company to rely on independent third-party audits of smelters and refiners rather than commissioning its own. Reliance of that kind is defensible only if the conformance status is verified at the time and the verification is recorded; this module supplies both, and in the DPMS context it marks the difference between sourcing on evidence and sourcing on reputation.

Lookups and status changes arrive as unreviewed items, are examined in review and, where a facility's standing is ambiguous or recently lapsed, are escalated pending decision before completion; closed items remain on the board's archive. The module operates on its assigned control cadence rather than a fixed calendar, because the trigger is the firm's own sourcing activity. Every lookup and disposition is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain kept under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, a daily attestation lands on the board at 06:00 UTC, and records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete footing. The firm can therefore show an inspector precisely which conformance status it saw, and relied upon, at the moment any sourcing decision was taken.
narratives.2.07:
Gold counterparties are verified before they are relied upon, and this module is the verification record. It tracks supply-chain declarations and Good Delivery assurance for the firm's gold counterparties, confirming each against LBMA Good Delivery standing and the requirements of LBMA Responsible Gold Guidance v9 before any reliance is placed. In a sector where provenance is the principal laundering exposure, the firm treats these checks as preconditions of business.

The Compliance Officer owns the module and its dispositions. Verification work enters the board as unreviewed arrivals and proceeds through review; a counterparty whose declarations are incomplete, or whose Good Delivery status cannot be confirmed, is escalated and held pending decision rather than waved through. Completion and closure follow only on a recorded outcome.

Operating on its assigned control cadence, the module re-examines counterparties as declarations are renewed or as LBMA standing changes, so assurance is a living position rather than an onboarding artefact. Three instruments anchor the control: LBMA Responsible Gold Guidance v9, the OECD DDG and Ministerial Decree 68/2024, which together carry the responsible-gold expectations the Ministry of Economy supervises in the DPMS sector.

For evidence, each verification, escalation and closure writes to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain the firm maintains under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24; the module board posts a daily integrity attestation at 06:00 UTC; and records are retained for five years, archived rather than deleted. The practical effect is that for any gold the firm has handled, we can evidence which counterparty supplied it, what that counterparty declared, and what assurance stood behind the declaration at the time it was accepted.
narratives.2.08:
Keeping the programme current is itself an obligation, and the firm manages regulatory change as a workflow rather than an ambient awareness. This module maintains the regulatory-change roadmap, supported by an AI-assisted implementation calendar, under a continuous horizon-scan: new instruments, amendments and supervisory guidance are captured as they emerge, not at periodic intervals. The scan spans federal legislation, Cabinet decisions, Ministry of Economy circulars and FATF outputs bearing on a DPMS reporting entity, and the MLRO and Compliance Officer own the module jointly.

The discipline rests on a single rule: every change captured receives an assigned owner and an implementation deadline at the point of capture. Items enter the board unreviewed, move into review where applicability and impact are assessed, and escalate to a pending-decision stage where scope or deadline is contested; completion records the implementation evidence and closure archives it. Nothing is closed on intention alone.

Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 and the FATF Methodology supply the basis, and both presume a programme that evolves with the obligations it implements; an out-of-date control set is a finding in waiting. The audit treatment is uniform with the rest of the estate. Each capture, assessment and closure is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, the board attests daily at 06:00 UTC, and records are retained for five years, archive-never-delete. When an inspector asks how the firm responded to a given circular, the module shows the date it was captured, who owned it, the deadline set, and the evidence on which it closed.
narratives.2.09:
Every consignment of bullion the firm handles is tracked from origin to vault in this module, which maintains the chain of custody and applies an AI-driven trade-based money-laundering scan at each custody transfer. Custody events are WORM-logged, so a transfer once recorded cannot be altered, and a consignment placed on hold has its settlement paused until the MLRO has reviewed and ruled. Day-to-day ownership sits with DPMS compliance; holds belong to the MLRO alone.

Operationally the workflow follows the metal. A shipment enters as a new consignment, passes a mandatory AML screen before movement, and travels onward to the vault; where the TBML scan or the screen raises concern, the consignment moves to held status and review is required before it can be cleared and delivered. The cadence is per consignment and per custody transfer. There is no sampling; every movement is an event in the record.

The basis is the supply-chain integrity and TBML expectation placed on the sector by MOE DPMS guidance, read with LBMA RGG v9 and the OECD 5-step framework. Trade-based laundering through mispriced or misdescribed bullion is the classic DPMS typology, and the module's premise is that custody evidence defeats it. Each custody event, scan result, hold and clearance writes to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24; the daily 06:00 UTC attestation posts to the module board; and records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted. A consignment's full journey can therefore be replayed end to end, with the reason for every hold and the authority for every release visible to the inspector.
narratives.2.10:
The Ministry of Economy's AML/CFT supervisory survey is a fixed obligation with a fixed deadline, and the firm manages it as exactly that. This module tracks the mandatory survey (reference MOEC/AML/001/2026), which applies to DNFBPs including dealers in precious metals and stones, from receipt of the request through to confirmed submission, with completion measured against the MOE deadline at every stage. The MLRO and the Compliance Officer share ownership, and the cadence follows the Ministry's own survey cycle: quiet between cycles, intensive within one. Treating a supervisory questionnaire as a workflow rather than a correspondence item is deliberate; the survey is the Ministry's principal sight-line into the firm between inspections, and the quality of the return is read, fairly or not, as a proxy for the quality of the programme.

Survey cycles open on the board as unreviewed arrivals; the questionnaire moves into review while responses are compiled and verified against source registers rather than drafted from memory, and contested answers escalate pending decision before the submission is completed and the cycle closed. Because survey answers are assertions made to the supervisor, the evidential trail matters as much as the submission itself. Every response compiled, every verification step and the submission record write to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain maintained under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24; the module board attests daily at 06:00 UTC; and records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted. If the Ministry later asks the firm to substantiate an answer, the module holds both the answer and the underlying material it was drawn from, cycle by cycle.
narratives.2.11:
Supervisory findings are tracked to closure in this module, not merely acknowledged. It serves as the firm's register of regulatory deadlines and corrective actions: each finding or undertaking received from a supervisor is decomposed into corrective actions, every action carries a named owner and a due date, and overdue items escalate automatically to the MLRO. The MLRO and the Compliance Officer hold joint ownership, the cadence is per finding and per deadline, and for a DPMS reporting entity the working assumption is that responsiveness to findings is itself examined. No finding ages without an owner.

An incoming finding lands on the board unreviewed and is scoped in review; where the remediation path or deadline is disputed, it escalates pending decision, and completion is recorded only against evidence that the corrective action was actually performed. Closure without evidence is not available in the workflow. Closed items move to the archive and stay there.

Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 grounds the obligation to remediate supervisory findings, and SOC2 CC7.4 supplies the incident-response discipline the firm applies to the same register. The evidentiary spine is uniform with the wider estate: every assignment, status change, escalation and closure writes to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, the daily attestation posts at 06:00 UTC on the module board, and records are retained for five years, archive-never-delete. A supervisor returning to a prior finding therefore sees the full remediation arc, including any deadline that slipped and the escalation it triggered, because a credible enforcement register records its own misses as faithfully as its closures.
narratives.2.12:
Senior oversight is exercised on the record, not in the corridor. This module carries the firm's board and management sign-off workflow: four-eyes approval of matters reserved to senior level, the minutes of governance committees, and the disposition of regulatory circulars requiring management attention. Two independent signatories are required on every sign-off, and a breached approval SLA escalates to the MLRO.

Ownership spans the Board, senior management and the MLRO, with a cadence of per approval alongside the periodic committee calendar. Matters enter as new items and pass under review; those requiring board authority queue awaiting board sign-off, and approval is recorded before the item is archived. Committee minutes follow the same path, so the governance record is a single register rather than a folder of documents of uncertain status.

The two-signatory rule is enforced in the workflow itself. A single officer cannot progress an item to approved status alone, whatever the urgency claimed, and the second signature must be independent of the first.

Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.20 and Cabinet 10/2019 Art.21 require senior management ownership of the AML/CFT programme, and inspection experience is blunt on the point: governance that cannot be evidenced is treated as governance that did not occur. Each item, signature, minute and disposition is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, with a daily attestation at 06:00 UTC on the module board, and records are retained for five years, archived rather than deleted. The Ministry of Economy can therefore trace any decision reserved to senior level from arrival to approval, with both signatories identified and the elapsed time against SLA visible on the face of the record.
narratives.2.13:
False positives are a compliance cost the firm manages deliberately, because an analyst hour spent eliminating a bad hit is an hour not spent on a real one. This module applies machine-learning pattern analysis to screening output, identifies recurring false-positive patterns and produces tuning proposals for screening thresholds. The word proposals is load bearing. Nothing the model produces changes a threshold by itself: every proposed change passes through four-eyes approval, with the Compliance Officer acting as maker and the MLRO as checker, before any tuning takes effect.

FDL 10/2025 Art.18 and FATF R.1 frame the control. The first imposes audit-trail and governance expectations on AI used in compliance decisioning; the second permits a risk-based approach on condition that calibration decisions are reasoned and documented. The module operates on its assigned control cadence: proposals arrive on the board unreviewed, are examined in review, escalate pending decision where maker and checker diverge, and complete only on the checker's recorded approval. A rejected proposal is preserved with its rejection reason, since declined changes evidence the discipline as well as adopted ones.

Every analysis run, proposal, approval and rejection writes to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24; the board attests daily at 06:00 UTC; and records are retained for five years, archive-never-delete. What an inspector sees is the full tuning history of the screening estate: what the model suggested, what the humans decided, and why. In a DPMS screening environment, where name transliteration and common trade names push hit rates upward, the firm regards documented tuning as the only defensible kind, and undocumented tuning as a control failure in its own right.
narratives.2.14:
Transaction-monitoring rules change only through this module, and only with evidence on both sides of the change. It manages the rule estate end to end across calibration reviews, change requests and approvals, on its assigned control cadence. The control is unambiguous: a rule change takes effect solely on recorded four-eyes approval, supported by before-and-after calibration evidence showing what the rule caught previously and what it is expected to catch afterwards. The Compliance Officer and the MLRO own the module jointly, and an undocumented rule change is, in inspection terms, indistinguishable from a control gap; the module exists to make such a change impossible in practice. Requests arrive on the board unreviewed, are analyzed in review with calibration evidence attached, escalate pending decision where approver and requester differ, and complete only on approval, with closed changes archived alongside their full evidence packet.

Cabinet 10/2019 Art.16 establishes the ongoing-monitoring obligation and FATF R.10/R.20 supply the international standard; both presume monitoring whose configuration is governed rather than improvised. The append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24 captures every request, calibration artefact, approval and deployment confirmation, the module board posts its daily attestation at 06:00 UTC, and records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis. The result is a monitoring estate whose present configuration can be reconciled, change by change, back to its origin, with a named approver at each step. For a DPMS firm whose transaction patterns shift with bullion flows and seasonal trade, calibration is frequent work, which is exactly why the firm refuses to let it become informal work.
narratives.2.15:
Findings raised by internal audit receive the same treatment the firm applies to supervisory findings: an owner, a due date and a tracked path to closure. This module is the internal-audit findings register. Each finding is logged with remediation ownership and a deadline, closure requires evidence rather than assertion, and any item that falls overdue escalates to senior management without negotiation. The Compliance Officer administers the register on its assigned control cadence, and the Board holds standing visibility of its contents.

Cabinet 10/2019 Art.21 requires an independent audit function and FATF R.18 carries the same expectation internationally. Independence is only worth what the institution does with its output, so the inspection question is rarely whether audits occurred but whether their findings changed anything; this register is the firm's proof that they do. Items arrive unreviewed, are validated in review, escalate pending decision where remediation scope is contested, and complete on evidenced closure before archiving.

Every status change, escalation and closure writes to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, with the daily 06:00 UTC attestation on the module board and five-year retention on an archive-never-delete basis. Because closures carry their evidence and overdue items carry their escalations, the register doubles as a measure of remediation culture. An inspector can see not only what internal audit found but how quickly the firm moved on it, and the Board sees precisely the same view we would put before the Ministry of Economy. Nothing in the register is curated for the audience reading it.
narratives.2.16:
Dormancy is treated by the firm as a risk state, not an administrative one. This module monitors dormant relationships and operates the reactivation review workflow, and its core control is categorical: a dormant relationship cannot transact again until customer due diligence has been refreshed and the refresh recorded. Reactivation requests therefore pass through compliance before any transaction, however small the amount or pressing the customer's stated need. The risk logic is conventional but real. Dormant relationships are an established re-entry route for illicit funds, because stale CDD and an absent transaction history blunt the monitoring that active relationships receive, and in the DPMS context a reactivated account can take delivery of high-value metal within days. Cabinet 10/2019 Art.7/16 grounds the control, pairing the ongoing-CDD obligation with the ongoing-monitoring obligation, and the module applies both at the reactivation moment, which is when they matter most.

The Compliance Officer owns the workflow, which operates on its assigned control cadence. Reactivation cases arrive on the board unreviewed, undergo review with the refreshed CDD attached, escalate pending decision where the refresh raises concern, and complete before closure to the archive. Each dormancy determination, reactivation request, CDD refresh and disposition writes to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, the daily attestation posts to the module board at 06:00 UTC, and records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis. The firm can therefore evidence, for any reactivated relationship, that fresh due diligence preceded the first new transaction, and an inspector can test that claim case by case from the chain rather than taking it on trust.
narratives.2.17:
Material outsourcing arrangements are recorded, risk-rated and reviewed in this register, on the principle that a delegated activity is never a delegated accountability. The module captures each third-party arrangement, assigns a risk rating, schedules its periodic review and holds a documented exit plan for every material arrangement, so the firm is never dependent on a provider it could not leave in an orderly way. Materiality is judged on the compliance significance of the function, not the contract value, and the Compliance Officer owns the register and its review cycle.

Cabinet 10/2019 Art.21 and the MOE DNFBP guidance supply the basis: internal controls must extend to functions performed by others on the firm's behalf, and the supervisor expects a DNFBP to know precisely which obligations travel with an outsourced service. Reviews run on the module's assigned control cadence, with arrangements re-examined on renewal, on incident, or on material change in a provider's circumstances.

New arrangements and review cycles arrive on the board unreviewed, progress through review, and escalate pending decision where a risk rating or the adequacy of an exit plan is contested; completion and closure follow the recorded outcome. Every registration, rating, review and escalation writes to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, the board posts its daily attestation at 06:00 UTC, and records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted. When the Ministry of Economy asks who performs what for the firm, on what terms and under what oversight, the register answers from the record, including the reviews that found an arrangement wanting and the corrective steps that followed.
narratives.2.18:
Conflicts of interest are declared, assessed and dispositioned in this register, on the firm's standing instruction that an undeclared conflict is itself a breach. Declarations are captured from staff and senior management as they arise, each is assessed by the Compliance Officer for compliance significance, and a recorded disposition follows in every case.

A conflict that cannot be resolved at officer level does not linger. It escalates to senior management, with the Managing Director as the named escalation point, and the escalation is recorded with the same care as the original declaration.

Cabinet 10/2019 Art.21 and FDL 10/2025 Art.20 ground the register within the firm's internal-control and governance obligations. In the DPMS sector the realistic conflict surface includes related-party trading, personal dealing in metal and overlapping directorships, and the register is examined with that surface in mind rather than as a formality. Declarations arrive on the board unreviewed, are examined in review, escalate pending decision where a disposition or its safeguards are disputed, and complete on a recorded outcome before closure. The register operates on its assigned control cadence, and standing declarations are revisited rather than left to age.

Evidence follows the estate-wide pattern. Every declaration, assessment, escalation and disposition writes to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24; the module board carries the daily 06:00 UTC attestation; and records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted. The firm can therefore show an inspector both the conflicts it identified and the discipline applied to each, including those refused, which is where a conflicts register earns its keep.
narratives.2.19:
Where the firm identifies a matter warranting proactive notification to its supervisory authority, the Voluntary Disclosure module governs the drafting, approval and submission tracking of that disclosure from first instruction through to regulator acknowledgement. The module gives effect to the firm's regulator cooperation duties under FDL 10/2025 and to MOE supervisory expectations, and it treats candour towards the supervisor as a controlled, evidenced process rather than ad hoc correspondence.

No disclosure leaves the firm on a single signature. Every draft requires approval by both the MLRO and senior management before submission, and the evidence of submission, including the channel used and the date of dispatch, is retained against the record. Ownership of the module sits jointly with the MLRO and senior management, who together decide whether a matter proceeds to disclosure, and the control operates per the applicable control cadence so that pending items do not age unattended. On the module's working board, a new matter enters as an unreviewed arrival and moves into review for drafting and assessment; where the decision to disclose is contested it rests in an escalated pending-decision state until the approving officers resolve it, after which the completed submission is closed with its acknowledgement evidence attached.

Every action taken in this module, from first draft to final approval, is written to the firm's append-only, hash-linked audit chain maintained under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries a daily attestation at 06:00 UTC confirming the chain's integrity. Disclosure records are retained for ten years on an archive-never-delete basis, so the full decision history behind every voluntary disclosure, including matters considered and ultimately not disclosed, remains producible to an inspector on request. We regard that completeness as the substance of cooperation: the supervisor sees not only what was sent, but how the firm decided to send it.
narratives.2.20:
Continuous measurement is how the firm satisfies itself that its analytical brain remains fit for purpose. The Eval KPIs module tracks model and brain evaluation indicators, covering calibration, drift and the effectiveness of individual reasoning modes, and tests each against the governance thresholds the firm has set. Its legal anchor is the ongoing AI performance assurance obligation in Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.18, which we read as requiring not a one-off validation exercise but a standing evidence stream showing that the models in production behave as approved. The module runs continuously, with results assessed per evaluation window, and is owned by the AI governance owner, who carries material deterioration into the board's escalation stage for a recorded decision.

Where a metric breaches its governance threshold, the breach is flagged for review rather than silently absorbed. On the module's board the finding arrives unreviewed, moves into review by the AI governance owner, and if material it is held in an escalated pending-decision state until a documented disposition is reached; completed findings are then closed with their analysis attached. Because these indicators bear on AI systems whose outputs can be adverse or consequential, any remediation arising from a breach carries human-in-the-loop review under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.18, and the models concerned remain governed through the firm's model registry, each carrying its risk tier and approval. Every evaluation result, threshold breach and reviewer decision is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and a daily attestation at 06:00 UTC on the module's board confirms the chain remains intact. KPI records are retained for ten years on an archive-never-delete basis, giving the supervisor a continuous performance history for every model the firm has operated.

GOVERNANCE & AUDIT
narratives.3.01:
Decision support for the Money Laundering Reporting Officer is delivered through the MLRO Advisor, an AI advisory capability that can be configured in three postures: executor, advisor or challenger. In each posture the module produces analysis with a full audit trail; in none of them does it decide. The MLRO retains decision authority over every matter on which the Advisor is consulted, and the module is invoked on demand rather than running autonomously. That division of labour reflects its legal basis: decision support preserving MLRO independence under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.18/24 and Cabinet 10/2019 Art.20.

We are deliberate about the limits of this tool. Where its output bears adversely or consequentially on a subject, the recommendation passes through human-in-the-loop review under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.18 before it informs any disposition, and the underlying models are governed through the firm's model registry with risk tier and approval recorded for each. Advisory sessions surface on the module's board as unreviewed arrivals and move into review by the MLRO; where a recommendation is contested or material, it is held in an escalated pending-decision state until the MLRO records a reasoned conclusion, after which the matter is completed and closed.

The evidentiary record is complete by construction. Each advisory request, the context supplied, the output returned and the MLRO's resulting decision are written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the board carries its daily attestation at 06:00 UTC confirming chain integrity. Records are retained for ten years and archived, never deleted. An inspector can therefore see, for any decision in which the Advisor played a part, exactly what the machine suggested, what the officer concluded and where the two diverged. Ownership and escalation rest with the MLRO alone, preserving the independence the law requires.
narratives.3.02:
Artificial intelligence at Hawkeye Sterling operates under an ethics framework, not on its own recognizance. The Responsible AI module governs every AI capability on the platform against the firm's published principles, with mandatory human oversight on adverse dispositions: no customer or subject receives an adverse outcome on machine output alone.

Two disciplines anchor the module. First, human-in-the-loop review applies to every adverse decision, consistent with Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.18, so consequential outputs are confirmed by an accountable officer before they take effect. Second, the model registry carries a risk tier and approval status for every model in production; nothing unapproved serves regulated work, and model changes re-enter governance review before release. The legal and standards basis is AI governance and accountability under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, read alongside the EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 42001, to which the firm's management system is aligned.

The control runs continuously and attaches to each individual AI decision. Joint ownership sits with the MLRO and the AI governance owner, and contested matters escalate between them through the board's pending-decision stage. On the working board, a flagged AI decision arrives unreviewed, is examined in review, and either completes with the reviewer's confirmation or is escalated for a senior pending decision before closure.

Every oversight action, override and approval is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, with the module's daily attestation at 06:00 UTC confirming the chain holds. Records are retained for ten years, archived and never deleted. We make no claim of autonomous compliance; the module exists precisely so that the human officer, not the model, remains the decision-maker of record, and so that an inspector can verify the oversight occurred in every case rather than accept that it generally does.
narratives.3.03:
Examination readiness is treated at this firm as a standing condition rather than a scramble. The Inspection Room assembles the regulator-ready evidence pack on demand: policies, the enterprise-wide risk assessment, case files, the audit chain itself and training records, aggregated through a one-click production control so that what reaches the Ministry of Economy is complete and internally consistent. The module answers the firm's examination readiness and record production duties to the MOE under Cabinet 10/2019 Art.24 and Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025.

Production follows a fixed path. A request enters the board as a new handoff, the pack is built during evidence pack assembly, and nothing leaves the firm until it has passed MLRO review; only then is the pack recorded as delivered to the regulator and, upon acknowledgement, closed. The MLRO owns the module outright and is the sole officer who releases evidence to an inspector, which keeps record production a controlled act rather than a clerical one. The cadence is on demand and per examination, so the module is exercised whenever the supervisor calls and can also be run internally to confirm the pack assembles cleanly.

Because the Inspection Room produces evidence about evidence, its own conduct is held to the standard it serves. Every assembly, review and delivery event is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries the daily 06:00 UTC attestation of chain integrity. Packs and their production records are retained for ten years on an archive-never-delete basis. An inspector who asks what was produced in an earlier examination, and under whose review it left the firm, receives a precise and verifiable answer.
narratives.3.04:
Sound dispositions begin with the text of the obligation. The Regulatory Library gives the compliance function a searchable reference covering UAE, FATF and Ministry of Economy material, with each instrument tagged to the frameworks it serves, so an officer reasoning through a case can cite the live obligation rather than a remembered paraphrase. Its basis is practical: access to current obligations supporting decisions, grounded in the FATF Methodology and MOE guidance, both of which expect a decision to be traceable to the rule applied.

The control is citation discipline. References drawn from the library are attached to dispositions, which means the regulatory footing of a decision travels with the decision itself and can be examined years later. The library operates continuously and is owned by the Compliance Officer, who maintains its currency and escalates interpretive questions through the board's escalation stage where guidance is ambiguous or newly issued. On that board, an incoming instrument or amendment arrives unreviewed, is assessed in review for its effect on the firm's obligations, and is completed and closed once its tagging and any downstream policy impact are settled.

Library activity is evidenced like everything else on the platform. Updates, tagging decisions and the citations bound into dispositions are written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's daily attestation at 06:00 UTC confirms the chain's integrity each morning. Records are retained for five years and archived rather than deleted. The practical effect, which we regard as the point of the module, is that an inspector reviewing any disposition can open the exact regulatory text the officer relied upon, in the version current when the decision was made.
narratives.3.05:
Our AML/CFT programme is a written, approved and versioned instrument, not an oral tradition. The Policies & SOPs module holds the programme charter and its supporting procedures, with each document versioned and bound to the audit chain so that the policy estate in force on any given date can be reconstructed exactly. The requirement for a documented, approved AML/CFT programme flows from Cabinet 10/2019 Art.21 and Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module is built to make that documentation demonstrable rather than asserted.

Two controls matter most. Every compliance decision taken on the platform references the policy version in force at the moment of decision, which closes the familiar examination gap between what the policy said and what the officer actually applied. And the estate is reviewed at least annually, with ownership running from the MLRO, who maintains and proposes, to the Board, which approves; that line of accountability is itself part of the control. Proposed changes enter the working board as unreviewed arrivals, are considered in review, and material amendments sit in an escalated pending-decision state until Board approval is minuted, after which the new version is completed, published and closed.

Each version event, review and approval is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the board carries its daily attestation at 06:00 UTC confirming integrity. Policy records are retained for ten years on an archive-never-delete basis. Superseded versions are archived, never destroyed, because the firm's position is that an inspector examining a historic case must be able to read the procedure as it stood then, alongside the evidence that it was approved and in force.
narratives.3.06:
Detection logic at the firm is anchored in recognized typologies rather than intuition. The Typology Library holds more than five hundred money-laundering and terrorist-financing typologies, searchable with AI assistance and contextualized for the UAE and for the dealers-in-precious-metals-and-stones sector in which we operate. Its purpose is typology-informed detection, drawing on the FATF Typologies and the MOE DPMS red-flags, so that what the platform flags can be traced to a pattern the international and domestic standard-setters have described. In operation the control is simple and strict: typology references are attached to detections, meaning that when the platform raises a concern, the typology said to be in play is named on the record, disciplining both the detection logic and the analyst's subsequent reasoning.

The library runs continuously under the ownership of the Compliance Officer, who curates additions, retires patterns that no longer reflect observed risk, and escalates contested entries through the board's pending-decision stage. New typology candidates arrive on the board unreviewed, are assessed in review against source material and sector relevance and are completed and closed once accepted into the corpus. The AI search layer assists retrieval only; it proposes nothing adverse of its own motion, and any consequential use of its output within a case remains subject to human-in-the-loop review under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.18, with the underlying models governed through the firm's registry by risk tier and approval. Curation events and the bindings between typologies and detections are written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, the module's board carries its daily attestation at 06:00 UTC, and records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted, preserving the evidential link between every detection and the typology that justified it.
narratives.3.07:
Procedural drift is a quiet way for an AML programme to fail, and the Playbook module exists to prevent it. Step-by-step AML/CFT playbooks govern the firm's recurring procedures, and each step, as it executes, writes its own audit entry. Mandated steps cannot be skipped silently; an omission is itself a recorded event requiring justification, which converts procedure from guidance into control. The module operates per procedure, engaged whenever a playbook-governed process runs, and is owned jointly by the Compliance Officer and the MLRO, with the MLRO taking escalations where execution departs from the mandated path.

The basis for this discipline is the expectation of consistent, evidenced execution under Cabinet 10/2019 and the FATF Methodology, both of which assess not whether a firm has procedures but whether it can show they were followed. On the working board, a procedure instance arrives unreviewed and progresses through review as its steps complete; any deviation or blocked step moves it into an escalated pending-decision state until the owning officers resolve it, and closure follows only when the full step record is intact.

Evidence is the module's product as much as execution is. Every step entry, skip justification and escalation is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries the daily attestation at 06:00 UTC confirming chain integrity. Playbook records are retained for ten years on an archive-never-delete basis. When an inspector asks how a regulated procedure was actually carried out on a particular occasion, the firm produces the step-by-step record of that specific run, not a copy of the procedure document and an assurance that it was followed.
narratives.3.08:
Persons whose data the firm processes hold rights of access and correction, and the Corrections module is the controlled channel through which those rights are honoured. Each data-subject request is logged on receipt, handled to resolution and closed within the statutory window, with the deadline tracked from the moment the request arrives. The legal foundation is the data-protection rights regime of the UAE PDPL, and the module's cadence follows it directly: per request, against the statutory deadline.

Handling is deliberately unexceptional. A request enters the board as an unreviewed arrival and moves into review, where identity is verified and the records in question are located; where the request is contested, touches regulated AML records or raises a tipping-off concern, it is escalated into a pending-decision state for senior determination before any response is issued. Joint ownership rests with the Compliance Officer and the Data Protection Officer, who between them resolve the tension that occasionally arises between a subject's correction rights and the firm's duty to preserve compliance records intact; where correction is warranted, the record is amended in a manner that preserves the original entry, consistent with the firm's archive-never-delete discipline.

The module's own conduct is fully evidenced. Receipt, verification, the decision taken and the response dispatched are each written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries its daily attestation at 06:00 UTC confirming the chain is intact. Request records are retained for five years and archived rather than deleted. The firm can therefore demonstrate not only that requests are answered in time, but that every step of each answer was taken by an identified officer under an auditable discipline.
narratives.3.09:
When an AI system fails, the firm does not improvise. The AI Incident Playbook provides the structured response to AI failures: containment first, root-cause analysis second, and mandatory reporting where thresholds are met, with disclosure to be made within 72 hours under the AI incident governance and disclosure ≤72h requirement of Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24. The module is engaged per incident, and the reporting clock is tracked on the record from the moment an incident is opened.

Two registers travel with the playbook. The Shadow-AI register captures unauthorized AI usage discovered during or outside incident response, and the Vendor-AI audit holds the due-diligence position of third-party AI suppliers, so an incident can be situated immediately against what the firm knew of the tool involved.

Ownership is shared between the MLRO and the AI governance owner. Between them they direct containment, approve the root-cause finding and decide on reporting, with contested judgements held in the board's escalated pending-decision state until resolved. An incident arrives on the board unreviewed, is worked in review through containment and analysis, and closes only when remediation and any required disclosure are complete.

Because the subject matter is AI, the playbook applies the firm's wider discipline. Consequential decisions arising from an incident, including any adverse effect on a customer or subject, carry human-in-the-loop review under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.18, and affected models are re-examined against the model registry's risk tier and approval before returning to service. Every containment step, finding and report is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, attested daily at 06:00 UTC on the module's board, and retained for ten years on an archive-never-delete basis, so the firm's response to its own failures is as inspectable as the failures themselves.
narratives.3.10:
Enterprise governance of artificial intelligence at Hawkeye Sterling is organized through a single framework module rather than scattered practice. It holds the stakeholder matrix, the AI risk register and the firm's mapping to NIST AI RMF and MITRE ATLAS, so that accountability, identified risks and the threat model for our AI estate sit in one examined place. The framework gives effect to the AI governance and audit trail obligations of FDL 10/2025 Art.18/24, is aligned to ISO/IEC 42001, and follows an EU AI Act-aligned posture for risk classification.

The central control is registry discipline. Every production model carries a risk tier, an approval and a model card, and no change to a model reaches production without passing governance review; the framework is the forum in which that review happens. Ownership rests with the MLRO together with the AI governance committee, operating per the applicable control cadence, and matters the committee cannot settle at first sight are escalated through the board's pending-decision stage for a recorded determination. A proposed model change or newly identified AI risk arrives on the board unreviewed, is examined in review against the risk register and the framework mappings and is completed and closed once the registry reflects the approved position.

We are careful not to overstate what the machines do. Adverse or consequential AI outputs across the estate carry human-in-the-loop review under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.18; the framework exists to make that oversight systematic rather than incidental. Every governance decision, registry change and risk-register entry is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, attested daily at 06:00 UTC on the module's board, and retained for ten years, archived and never deleted, so the supervisor can trace any model in production back to the committee decision that admitted it.
narratives.3.11:
Unauthorized AI tooling is treated here as a compliance finding, not an IT nuisance. The Shadow AI Register detects and remediates AI tools in use without authorization, including vendors engaged without a data-processing agreement, because an ungoverned model handling firm or customer data sits outside every control the programme depends on. The register's legal footing is twofold: the AI governance requirements of FDL 10/2025 Art.18 and the data-protection obligations of the UAE PDPL, both of which are engaged the moment data reaches a tool the firm has not approved.

Each discovery is logged; risk-classified and remediated, and repeat findings escalate; a function that returns to an unauthorized tool after remediation is not given the benefit of the doubt a first finding receives.
