The Compliance Officer owns the register, operates it per the applicable control cadence, and carries escalations into the board's pending-decision stage where remediation is resisted or the exposure is material. On that board, a suspected shadow tool arrives unreviewed, is investigated in review to establish what data it touched and under what terms and is closed only when the tool is either retired or brought inside the governance perimeter with proper approvals. Tools that are regularized then enter the model registry with a risk tier and approval like any other, and any adverse or consequential output they go on to produce remains subject to human-in-the-loop review under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.18.

Findings, classifications and remediation outcomes are written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries the daily 06:00 UTC attestation of chain integrity. Register records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis, so the firm's history of discovering and correcting its own ungoverned AI use remains visible to the supervisor in full.
narratives.3.12:
No AI vendor serves this firm on reputation alone. The Vendor AI Audit module holds the due-diligence file for every third-party AI supplier: the data-processing agreement, the model card, penetration-test evidence and the service-level agreement, assembled per vendor before any service is consumed. Onboarding is conditional, and the condition is complete evidence; a vendor whose file lacks any element is not onboarded, and an onboarded vendor is re-audited annually so the file cannot quietly go stale. Third-party AI risk is the module's subject, and its basis is FDL 10/2025 Art.18, ISO/IEC 42001 and the UAE PDPL, the last of which governs the data-processing terms in particular.

The Compliance Officer owns the audit, operating per the applicable control cadence, and escalates deficient or expired evidence through the board's pending-decision stage, where continued use of the vendor is decided on the record. A vendor file arrives on the board unreviewed at onboarding or re-audit, is examined in review against the evidence requirements, and is completed and closed when the file stands complete; deficiencies hold the file open until cured or the relationship ends. Vendor models that reach production are also entered in the firm's model registry, carrying a risk tier and approval, and any adverse or consequential output they contribute to remains subject to human-in-the-loop review under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.18.

Every onboarding decision, evidence receipt and re-audit outcome is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, attested daily at 06:00 UTC on the module's board. Records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted, so the diligence behind every AI supplier the firm has used is producible end to end, including suppliers declined and relationships exited.
narratives.4.01:
Enhanced due diligence often turns on what the open record reveals. The OSINT module harvests open-source signals, including media, public registries and other lawfully accessible material, when a subject's risk profile triggers enhanced due diligence, and its findings are attached directly to the subject file so they form part of the assessable record rather than an analyst's loose notes. The module is engaged on EDD trigger, not run indiscriminately, and is owned by the Compliance Officer together with the investigator handling the case, with the Compliance Officer carrying escalations where findings change the subject's risk posture.

The legal anchor is the enhanced due diligence obligation under FATF R.10 and Cabinet 10/2019 Art.15, which require the firm to deepen its understanding of higher-risk subjects beyond standard identification. In practice an OSINT task arrives on the module's board unreviewed, is worked in review as sources are gathered and weighed, and where the material discovered is adverse or ambiguous it moves into an escalated pending-decision state for a senior view before the subject file is updated; completed tasks are then closed with their source captures preserved.

Discipline around provenance matters as much as discovery. Each harvested finding, its attachment to the subject file and the reviewing officer's conclusion are written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries the standing daily attestation at 06:00 UTC. Records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis. The result is that an inspector examining any EDD file can see not only what the open record showed but when it was found, by whom, and how it altered the firm's assessment of the subject.
narratives.4.02:
Counterparty identification begins, where the counterparty is a legal entity, with the Legal Entity Identifier. The GLEIF / LEI module performs LEI lookups and counterparty name searches against the Global LEI Foundation's reference data, verifying that the entity the firm believes it is dealing with corresponds to a registered, identifiable legal person. Where an LEI is available it is captured into the counterparty record as a matter of control, giving later reviewers an unambiguous identifier rather than a name string open to confusion.

This control serves the counterparty identification expectations of FATF R.16, which concern themselves with the accuracy of party information accompanying transactions; an authoritative entity identifier captured at the outset is the cheapest defense against later misattribution. The module operates at onboarding and on demand thereafter, under the ownership of the Compliance Officer, who escalates unresolved or conflicting identity results through the board's escalation stage rather than letting an uncertain match pass into the relationship. A lookup task arrives on the board unreviewed, is examined in review where results are compared against the counterparty's claimed particulars, and completes and closes once the identifier, or the documented absence of one, is recorded on the file.

Verification events are evidenced permanently. Each search, the result returned and the officer's recording decision are written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries its daily attestation at 06:00 UTC confirming the chain's integrity. Records are retained for five years and archived, never deleted. The firm can therefore show, for any counterparty, the identity evidence held at onboarding and every subsequent occasion on which it was refreshed or challenged.
narratives.4.03:
Opaque ownership is where beneficial owners hide, and the Entity Graph is the firm's instrument for seeing through it. The module renders relationship and ownership networks as a graph, allowing an investigator to trace shareholding chains, common directorships and informal connections across the entities surrounding a subject. It is deployed on complex-structure review, when standard due diligence meets a structure whose ownership cannot be read from a single registry extract, and its findings feed the enhanced due diligence of those entities.

Understanding control structures is the obligation in play, anchored in FATF R.24/25 and Cabinet 58/2020, which together require the firm to establish who ultimately owns and controls its customers and counterparties.

The graph is owned jointly by the investigator and the MLRO; the investigator builds and interprets, while the MLRO takes the escalation where the structure resolves to a concerning controller or refuses to resolve at all. A complex-structure case arrives on the module's board unreviewed, is developed in review as the network is mapped, and passes through an escalated pending-decision state where the ownership conclusion carries consequence; it closes once the structure, as understood, is recorded on the subject file.

The mapping work itself is evidenced, not just its conclusion.

Graph constructions, the sources relied upon and the ownership determinations reached are written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, with the daily 06:00 UTC attestation standing on the module's board. Records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis, so the reasoning by which the firm concluded who stood behind a structure remains open to inspection long after the case has closed.
narratives.4.04:
A subject's web presence is evidence, and the firm reads it methodically. The Domain Intel module gathers intelligence on domains and web infrastructure connected to a subject, including registration particulars, hosting posture and an assessment of email-spoofing risk, and records its findings in the subject file where they inform the wider risk picture. It is invoked on EDD trigger, as one of the enhanced measures available when a subject's profile demands more than standard diligence, and its basis lies in the EDD and fraud-risk signal expectations of FATF R.10. Web-facing indicators are treated with the same care as registry evidence, because in this sector a polished site can substitute for substance.

The investigator owns the module and works each task personally.

A domain enquiry arrives on the board unreviewed and is examined in review as infrastructure is enumerated and spoofing exposure assessed; findings suggesting impersonation, fabricated substance or fraud risk are escalated into the pending-decision state for senior consideration before the file is concluded and closed. Short of escalation, the recorded output is the same in kind: a documented infrastructure picture attached to the subject and available to every later reviewer of that file.

Every enquiry, finding and attachment is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries the daily attestation at 06:00 UTC confirming chain integrity. Records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted. When an inspector asks why the firm doubted a counterparty's bona fides, or why it did not, the infrastructure evidence considered at the time is producible exactly as the investigator saw it.
narratives.4.05:
Virtual-asset exposure enters the firm's risk model through a dedicated scoring control. The Crypto Risk module assesses wallet and virtual-asset exposure for any subject with a virtual-asset link, producing an exposure score that informs the subject's overall risk rating rather than sitting beside it as commentary. The cadence follows the risk: the module is engaged whenever a virtual-asset-linked subject is identified, whether at onboarding or in later review, and its basis is the virtual-asset exposure assessment expectation of FATF R.15 read with VARA's framework in this jurisdiction.

Scoring is owned by the Compliance Officer, who reviews each assessment and escalates material exposure through the board's pending decision stage, where the consequence for the relationship is decided on the record. An assessment arrives on the module's board unreviewed, moves into review as wallet exposure is analyzed and the score settled, and completes and closes once the rating consequence is recorded on the subject file; contested or borderline scores rest in escalation until determined. The score itself is an input to human judgement, not a verdict, and any adverse disposition that follows from it is taken by an accountable officer rather than by the scoring logic.

Each assessment, the score produced and its effect on the risk rating are written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries the standing daily attestation at 06:00 UTC. Records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis. The firm can therefore evidence, for every virtual-asset-linked subject, what exposure was found, how it was scored and how the rating responded, which is the level of granularity we expect a supervisor to test.
narratives.4.06:
Trade exposure brings maritime risk, and the firm screens for it directly. The Vessel Check module screens vessels connected to a transaction or relationship against sanctions designations and dark-fleet indicators, addressing the exposure that arises when goods move by sea under flags and operators that may conceal sanctioned interests. Screening runs on trade-related screening events, and its basis is the sanctions screening regime of Cabinet 74/2020, the OFAC SDN list and FATF R.6.

Hits are not triaged casually. A vessel sanctions or dark-fleet hit is routed to the MLRO, which is the control's defining feature: the decision on a maritime sanctions exposure is taken at reporting-officer level, not at the screening desk. Ownership is shared between the Compliance Officer, who operates the screening, and the MLRO, who determines hits.

On the module's board a screening result arrives unreviewed and is examined in review against vessel identity and voyage context. A confirmed or unresolved hit moves into the escalated pending-decision state for the MLRO's determination, and only then is the matter completed and closed.

Sanctions decisions are precisely the records an inspector will test, so the module's evidence is kept beyond argument. Every screening run, hit, routing and determination is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, with the module's daily attestation at 06:00 UTC confirming the chain's integrity. Records are retained for five years and archived rather than deleted. For any vessel the firm has screened, the listing position at the time, the analyst's working and the MLRO's conclusion are producible as a single tamper-evident thread, which is how we expect a maritime sanctions file to be read.
narratives.4.07:
Fabricated numbers betray themselves statistically, and the firm exploits that fact. The Benford Analysis module applies Benford's-law testing to transaction datasets, examining whether leading-digit distributions depart from the pattern genuine financial activity tends to follow. Departures do not constitute suspicion by themselves; they feed investigation, directing analytical attention towards datasets whose shape suggests manipulation, structuring or invented values.

That feeding of investigation is the module's control, and its basis is the use of transaction analytics in forming suspicion under FATF R.20.

The analyst owns the module and runs it on dataset analysis, typically when a portfolio, customer book or transaction extract warrants statistical examination. Results arrive on the module's board unreviewed and are interpreted in review, where each anomaly is sized against legitimate explanations such as pricing conventions or rounding practice; findings that survive that scrutiny are escalated into the pending-decision state so that a senior officer decides on the record whether an investigation opens. Analyses close with their conclusions documented whether or not suspicion crystallized, because a negative result is itself evidence of monitoring performed.

Each analysis, its parameters, the anomalies identified and the decisions taken on them are written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries the daily attestation at 06:00 UTC confirming the chain stands unbroken. Records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis. An inspector reviewing the firm's analytical monitoring can therefore see not only the alerts the statistics produced but the reasoning applied to each, including those judged innocuous.
narratives.4.08:
When an alert ripens into a case, the Investigation module becomes the firm's working file: a case workbench in which every document, screening extract and analyst note is lodged in an evidence vault and ordered on a single case timeline. The discipline applied is deliberately plain. If material informed a decision, it sits in the vault; if an event moved the case forward, it appears on the timeline. Cabinet 10/2019 expects suspicious transaction reporting to rest on structured, evidenced investigation, and this module is where that expectation is discharged in practice: the STR decision the MLRO ultimately takes can be traced back through the assembled record without reconstruction.

The module operates per case. Matters arrive in the workbench as unreviewed new arrivals and are taken into review by the assigned investigator; where the facts support escalation, the case moves to a pending-decision state in which the MLRO weighs the evidence before completion and closure. Ownership is shared. The Investigator builds the record, the MLRO owns escalation and the reporting decision, and nothing leaves the escalated state without that judgement being recorded.

Every action taken in the module, from evidence lodged to state transitions and final decisions, is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain maintained under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries a daily attestation at 06:00 UTC confirming the control operated as designed. Case records are retained for five years and are archived rather than deleted at the end of that period, so a closed investigation remains producible to the Ministry of Economy or the FIU in precisely the form in which it was decided. We regard this module as the connective tissue between detection and reporting: the place where suspicion either earns its escalation on evidence or is set aside with the reasons preserved.
narratives.4.09:
Country risk scoring sits at the front of our customer due diligence. The Single Country module assembles a jurisdiction's money-laundering and terrorist-financing profile from the Basel AML Index, the Corruption Perceptions Index, FATF listings, sanctions exposure and stability indicators, and converts that composite into a single score that drives the level of CDD applied. The consequence is mechanical by design: the country score determines whether a relationship receives standard due diligence, enhanced due diligence, or enhanced measures with senior approval. Analyst discretion cannot downgrade the outcome the score dictates.

Assessment occurs at onboarding and at every periodic review, while the underlying lists are refreshed continuously, so a deterioration in a jurisdiction's standing reaches our risk decisions without waiting for the next review date. The Compliance Officer owns the module day to day and escalates contested or borderline outcomes to the MLRO, who holds final authority on the CDD level applied. Assessments enter the module queue as unreviewed arrivals, pass through review, and where judgement is required are escalated pending decision before completion and closure.

This treatment of geographic risk gives effect to FATF R.19, to Cabinet 10/2019 and to MOE high-risk guidance, each of which requires jurisdiction risk to operate as a live input to the risk-based approach rather than an annual footnote. Every scoring run, list refresh and CDD determination is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain kept under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24; a daily attestation posts to the module's board at 06:00 UTC; and records are held for five years on an archive-never-delete basis. The score that governed any onboarding decision can therefore be reproduced exactly as it stood on the day, together with the list states that produced it.
narratives.4.10:
Geopolitical events move faster than review calendars, and this module exists to close that gap. It carries a live feed of geopolitical developments, assesses each material event for portfolio impact, and overlays the result on the firm's risk map so the Compliance Officer can see at once which relationships are exposed to a conflict, a change of regime or a sudden sanctions realignment. Where an event is material, the module routes its impact into the country-risk review process, so the jurisdiction score, and with it the CDD level, is reconsidered on the strength of assessed facts rather than headlines.

The module operates at the cadence set for this control in the compliance calendar, with the feed itself running live beneath it. Incoming events queue as unreviewed arrivals; the Compliance Officer takes material items into review and escalates those pending decision where portfolio exposure warrants MLRO attention, before the item is completed and closed. The Compliance Officer owns the module and answers for its escalations.

Treating geographic risk as dynamic rather than static is what FATF R.1/R.10 and Cabinet 10/2019 Art.4 require of a risk-based programme, and this module is the mechanism through which the firm meets that requirement between formal review dates. Each event assessment, impact determination and routing decision is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24. The module's board posts its attestation daily at 06:00 UTC. Records are retained for five years and archived thereafter, never deleted, preserving the link between a world event and the review it triggered. In inspection terms, that link is the value: we can show not only that we watched the world, but that watching changed what we did.
narratives.4.11:
A single heat-map of global jurisdiction risk anchors the firm's geographic risk assessment. The Risk Map module renders every country's composite standing in one view, and that view feeds directly into onboarding and enhanced due diligence decisions: when a prospective or existing relationship carries exposure to a jurisdiction shown as high risk, EDD is triggered under the country-risk methodology, not at anyone's option. The map is therefore less a visualization than a tripwire, the point at which geographic exposure becomes a due diligence obligation.

The legal foundation sits in FATF R.1/R.19 on higher-risk countries and in Cabinet 10/2019 Art.4, which together require geographic risk to be assessed and acted upon as part of the risk-based approach. The Compliance Officer owns the module, operates it at the cadence set for the control, and escalates contested exposure determinations to the MLRO. Map-driven findings enter the queue as unreviewed arrivals, move into review, and where an EDD determination is disputed or unusual are escalated pending decision before completion and closure.

Every change in a jurisdiction's banding, every exposure determination and every EDD trigger is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain maintained under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board attests daily at 06:00 UTC that the control ran. Records are kept for five years and then archived, never deleted. An inspector can therefore reconstruct, for any onboarding decision in the period, exactly how the world map looked when the decision was taken and why the EDD obligation did or did not arise.

We treat that reconstructability as the test of the control: a heat-map that cannot be replayed proves nothing, whereas this one carries its own history.
narratives.4.12:
Attempts to evade sanctions rarely announce themselves; they surface as patterns. The Sanctions Evasion module runs typology detection continuously across the firm's data, looking for recognized signatures of circumvention, and flags every matched pattern directly to the MLRO. The control is deliberately narrow and senior. Detection is automated and unceasing, but judgement on what a flagged pattern means belongs to the MLRO alone, who owns the module and all onward escalation, including any decision to open an investigation or make a report. Because detection runs continuously rather than on a cycle, the interval between an evasion pattern emerging and the MLRO seeing it is set by the feed, not by the review calendar.

Flagged patterns arrive as unreviewed items, are taken into review, and where the evidence is substantial move to an escalated, pending-decision state before completion and closure. Nothing is dismissed silently: a pattern set aside is closed with its reasoning on the record, and a pattern confirmed passes into the investigation workbench with its detection history intact. The obligations served are the sanctions-compliance regime under Cabinet 74/2020, OFAC exposure and FATF R.6, which together require not merely list screening but alertness to the structures used to defeat it. Screening answers whether a name is listed; this module addresses whether conduct suggests a party is organizing itself around the lists.

Every detection, review and disposition is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain kept under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, the module's board carries its daily 06:00 UTC attestation, and records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis, preserving both the patterns we acted upon and those we reasoned away.
narratives.4.13:
Investigative depth on higher-risk subjects comes from a bench of specialized instruments, and this module is that bench. Intelligence Tools brings together the UBO walker, crypto-exposure analysis and synthetic-identity detection in a single console, invoked whenever an EDD trigger fires on a subject. The governing discipline is evidential: whatever the tools produce is attached to the subject's file, so the enhanced measures applied to a customer are documented in the same place as the customer. Tool runs that go nowhere are recorded with the same care as tool runs that change a risk rating. In practice the three instruments are often run together on higher-risk subjects, since the typologies they address, layered ownership, virtual-asset exposure and fabricated identity, tend to travel in company.

The Investigator owns the module, runs it on EDD trigger rather than on a calendar, and escalates material findings to the MLRO through the standard case route. Findings enter as unreviewed arrivals and move into review; where a result is significant, say a previously undisclosed ownership layer or unexplained crypto exposure, the item is escalated pending decision before it completes and closes.

The legal footing is the enhanced due diligence and UBO-verification framework of FATF R.10/24 and Cabinet 58/2020, which require deeper measures where risk is higher and proof that those measures were in fact performed. Every invocation, output and attachment is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board attests daily at 06:00 UTC. Records are retained for five years and pass to archive rather than deletion, preserving for inspection the full body of intelligence that stood behind each enhanced due diligence conclusion.
narratives.4.14:
Everything this platform decides leaves a permanent, verifiable mark, and the Audit Trail module is where those marks live. It maintains an immutable, tamper-evident chain of every decision: entries are hash-linked so that each record seals the one before it, the chain is append-only, integrity is verified on an ongoing basis, and a dedicated export produces the record in a form the FIU can consume. Operation is per event and continuous; there is no batching window in which an unrecorded decision can exist.

The chain is the firm's principal answer to the record-keeping and traceability duties of Cabinet 10/2019 Art.24 and Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24. Under the latter, every module on the platform writes its actions into this append-only, hash-linked chain, which makes the Audit Trail the evidentiary spine of the whole programme rather than one control among many. Its own operations, integrity verifications, exports and archival events, are chained in the same way, so the record of the record is itself tamper-evident.

The workflow is short by design: new events are written, sealed into the chain, and archived at year-end. Joint ownership sits with the MLRO and the CTO, the former accountable for what the chain must prove, the latter for the cryptographic and operational integrity with which it proves it. Suspected integrity faults escalate to both immediately.

A daily attestation posts to the module's board at 06:00 UTC confirming chain integrity. Retention runs to ten years on WORM storage, write-once and read-many, and the archive is never deleted: a sealed year remains exactly as sealed. We treat any proposal to amend the chain as a category error, since the control's entire value to a supervisor is that amendment is impossible and that every assertion the firm makes elsewhere in this file can be checked against it.
narratives.5.01:
Our awareness of the outside world runs through this feed. The module carries live UAE regulatory developments and adverse-media coverage in seven languages, refreshed every five minutes, with AI triage reading the stream so that nothing material waits on an analyst's reading list. Items the triage grades HIGH or CRITICAL are surfaced immediately and worked first; the remainder stays as searchable context. The Compliance Officer owns the feed, reviews what it surfaces, and escalates items with subject or portfolio impact to the MLRO.

Surfaced items land in the queue as unreviewed arrivals and are taken into review; where an item implicates an existing relationship or requires a programme response, it is escalated pending decision before completion and closure. Because the refresh interval is five minutes, the gap between publication of adverse news and its arrival in the queue is short enough that ongoing monitoring is genuinely ongoing rather than periodic in disguise.

The seven-language coverage matters in this market, where adverse reporting on a counterparty frequently appears first outside English-language sources.

Continuous adverse-media and negative-news monitoring is what Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 and FATF R.6 expect of the firm, and the feed is the standing mechanism by which that expectation is met. Every triage grade, review and disposition is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24; the module's board posts its attestation daily at 06:00 UTC; and feed records are retained for five years, archived at the end of the period and never deleted. An inspector reviewing any escalation can therefore see the original item, the machine triage that prioritized it and the human judgement that resolved it, in sequence and without gaps.
narratives.5.02:
Analysts working a case need somewhere to think on the record, and the Workbench is that place. It provides a structured analysis workspace in which a compliance analyst assembles hypotheses, working notes, extracts and intermediate conclusions, with the whole work product linked to the underlying case and preserved as evidence. The control point is exactly that linkage: analysis is not done in side documents or personal files, where it would be invisible to review, but inside a workspace whose contents attach to the case they concern and survive it. Cabinet 10/2019 Art.24's record-keeping and investigation-support requirements form the basis, and the article's assumption is the module's design brief, namely that a firm can show its working, with the path from raw material to conclusion preserved rather than the conclusion alone.

Work items enter the workspace as unreviewed arrivals, are progressed in review by the analyst, and are escalated pending decision when a product needs MLRO judgement before it completes and closes.

Ownership rests with the Compliance Analyst under MLRO oversight, and that oversight line is also the escalation route for contested or significant analytical products. The module operates at the cadence set for the control, in practice tracking the tempo of the casework it supports. Every workspace action, creation, amendment, linkage and closure, is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries a daily attestation at 06:00 UTC. Work products are retained for five years and archived thereafter, never deleted. The effect is that analytical judgement, often the least documented stage of compliance work in this sector, is here among the best evidenced, and an inspector can follow a thought from first note to final disposition.
narratives.5.03:
Telemetry gives the firm a live view of its own machinery. The module streams system telemetry and metrics for the compliance control plane, screening services, monitoring jobs and audit infrastructure alike, so the operational health of the controls is itself observed and evidenced. The discipline is simple: control-plane metrics are monitored, and anomalies alert operations rather than waiting to be noticed. A control that silently degrades is, for compliance purposes, a control that has failed; this module exists so that no such silence is possible.

Operations and the Compliance Officer own the module jointly, with operations answering for system health and the Compliance Officer for the compliance consequence of any degradation; sustained or material anomalies escalate through the incident route. Anomaly items queue as unreviewed arrivals, are taken into review, and are escalated pending decision where remediation requires a judgement call, closing only once resolved. The module operates at the cadence set for the control, with the underlying metric stream running continuously beneath it.

The basis is twofold: the operational-resilience criteria of SOC2 CC7, and the system-integrity expectations of FDL 10/2025, which treat the reliability of an AI-assisted compliance platform as part of its lawfulness.

Telemetry readings that evidence control health, together with every anomaly raised and resolved, are written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24. The module's board posts a daily attestation at 06:00 UTC. Records are retained for five years, archived at term and never deleted, so the firm can demonstrate not only that its controls existed but that they were observably up, watched and answered for on any given day within the retention window.
narratives.5.04:
We attack our own AI layer on a standing basis, before anyone hostile does. The Red-Team module is the adversarial probe console through which sixteen probes across six categories are run against the platform's AI components: prompt-injection attempts, manipulation of reasoning, attempts to extract or distort outputs, and related adversarial classes aligned to MITRE ATLAS.

The control is unambiguous about consequence. Probe results are reviewed, and a failed probe does not become a backlog ticket; it opens an AI incident under the AI incident playbook, with all that the playbook then requires. Probe outcomes enter the queue as unreviewed arrivals, are reviewed, and are escalated pending decision where a failure or borderline result requires an incident determination before closure.

Ownership is joint between the MLRO and security, reflecting the module's dual nature: it is a security exercise whose findings carry compliance consequence, and neither function can close a finding alone where the other's remit is engaged. The module operates at the cadence set for the control in the testing calendar. The legal context is FDL 10/2025 Art.18, which expects demonstrable adversarial resilience from AI used in regulated decisions, with MITRE ATLAS-aligned testing as the firm's chosen method of demonstration.

Every probe run, result, review and incident decision is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board attests daily at 06:00 UTC. Records are retained for ten years on an archive-never-delete basis, matching the AI governance horizon, so the full adversarial history of the platform, including what failed and what was done about it, remains available to inspection. We hold that an unflattering probe result preserved on the record is worth more to a supervisor than a flattering one recalled from memory.
narratives.5.05:
Security posture is tracked here as a single, current picture. The Security module is the audit dashboard on which control posture and audit results across the platform are consolidated: what is in place, what was tested, what was found, and where each finding now stands. Its operating rule is remediation-shaped. Findings are tracked to remediation rather than to acknowledgement, and critical findings do not queue; they escalate immediately.

Joint ownership sits with security and the Compliance Officer, the one accountable for technical remediation, the other for the compliance significance of what the dashboard shows, and the immediate-escalation path for criticals runs to both.

Findings enter as unreviewed arrivals and are taken into review; those needing a risk decision are escalated pending decision, and items complete and close only when remediation is evidenced. The module operates at the cadence set for the control, with the dashboard standing current between cycles.

The basis is SOC2 CC6/CC7 on logical access and operations, together with FDL 10/2025 Art.24, which ties the integrity of the compliance record to the security of the system that keeps it. That connection is why this dashboard sits in the inspection file at all: a firm cannot vouch for an audit chain it cannot show it protects. Every finding, status change and remediation closure is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24; the module's board carries its daily 06:00 UTC attestation; and records are retained for five years, archived thereafter and never deleted. The dashboard's history is therefore as inspectable as its present, and the firm's security claims rest on tracked findings rather than assertion.
narratives.5.06:
Availability is itself a compliance property when the services in question screen names and seal audit records. The Status module maintains live service status and health for the platform's compliance-critical services, producing availability evidence rather than uptime decoration. Its control is paired: availability is monitored through the module's health view, and when an incident occurs it is recorded together with recovery evidence in accordance with the incident runbook. Outages are therefore not embarrassments to be smoothed over but events to be timed, evidenced and learned from.

Operations owns the module and runs it at the cadence set for the control; incidents escalate through the runbook's defined route, which carries material events to compliance leadership. Status items and incident records enter the queue as unreviewed arrivals, move into review, and are escalated pending decision where an incident's classification or closure requires judgement before the matter completes.

The framework served is SOC2 CC7.4 on incident response and operational resilience, and the module supplies precisely the artefacts that criterion expects detection records, response timelines and recovery evidence. Every status event, incident record and recovery artefact is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, which prevents after-the-fact tidying of an incident narrative. The module's board posts a daily attestation at 06:00 UTC. Records are retained for five years and pass to archive rather than deletion, so the operational history of the platform, including its bad days, remains intact for supervisory review. We consider that intactness the point. Resilience claims are only as credible as the unedited record beneath them, and this module is where that record is kept.
narratives.5.07:
Documentation of the platform's integration surface is treated here as a control, not a courtesy. The API Docs module maintains the reference documentation for every interface through which the platform is reached, and its discipline is twofold: API changes are documented and versioned, so the integration surface has a history rather than merely a current state, and authentication requirements are stated per endpoint, so no consumer can plead ignorance of what access requires. An undocumented change to a compliance API is, in our assessment, an unmanaged change.

SOC2 CC8 on change management and access documentation supplies the basis. The criterion asks that changes be controlled and that access expectations be explicit, and this module is the artefact that proves both for the API estate. Operations owns it, keeps the documentation current at the cadence set for the control, and escalates discrepancies between documented and actual behaviour, which are treated as findings rather than typographical matters. Documentation items enter the queue as unreviewed arrivals, pass through review, and are escalated pending decision where a change's treatment is contested, before completion and closure.

Every documentation change and version event is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board attests daily at 06:00 UTC.

Records are retained for five years, archived at term and never deleted, which means the documentation as it stood at any past date can be produced alongside the conduct of integrators who relied on it. Modest as this module appears beside the screening controls, it carries weight in inspection: the integration surface is where the platform meets the outside world, and we can show it has never been an undescribed one.
narratives.5.08:
Transparency about the AI layer is a documented duty, and the System Card module is the document. It holds the model system card and governance disclosures for the platform's AI: which models are in service, their risk tiers, the approvals under which they operate, and the governance arrangements surrounding them. The control rule keeps the card honest. It is maintained current against the model registry, so disclosure tracks reality rather than the last annual refresh, and risk tiers and approvals are disclosed outright rather than merely held internally.

Ownership sits with the MLRO together with the AI governance committee, the committee being both the approver of model changes and the escalation destination when card and registry diverge. The module operates at the cadence set for the control. Proposed disclosure updates enter as unreviewed arrivals, pass through review, and are escalated pending decision where a disclosure question needs committee judgement before completion and closure.

FDL 10/2025 Art.18 grounds the duty in UAE law, and the documentation duties of ISO/IEC 42001 shape its form; between them they require an AI operator to state what it runs, under what controls, and on whose authority.

Every revision to the card and every governance disclosure is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, the module's board posts its attestation daily at 06:00 UTC, and the record is retained for ten years on an archive-never-delete basis. A supervisor can therefore read not only today's card but the card as it stood when any past model decision was taken, which is the difference between transparency and retrospective tidiness. We built the module to make the former demonstrable and the latter impossible.
narratives.5.09:
No release reaches production unexamined. The Security Scan module carries the platform's dependency and code security scanning, static analysis of the codebase and composition analysis of everything it imports, together with the remediation tracking that turns findings into closures. The gate is fail-closed and unambiguous: HIGH and CRITICAL findings block release until they are remediated or formally risk-accepted with sign-off. There is no third path, and no release manager can waive the gate informally.

Security and Operations own the module jointly, with escalation running to compliance leadership where a risk-acceptance is sought, since accepting a known weakness in a compliance platform is a governance decision rather than a build decision. Findings enter the queue as unreviewed arrivals and are taken into review; those proposed for risk-acceptance are escalated pending decision and close only with the sign-off on record.

The module operates at the cadence set for the control, aligned to the release rhythm it polices. Secure development under SOC2 CC8 and prevailing supply-chain security expectations form the basis: a platform that scrutinizes others must be able to show its own components are scrutinized, and that the scrutiny has teeth.

Every scan result, blocking decision, remediation and sign-off is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24; a daily attestation posts to the module's board at 06:00 UTC; and records are retained for five years, archived afterwards and never deleted. The inspection value lies in the chain of custody this creates for the software itself.

For any release in the period, the firm can produce what was found, who decided, and on what authority the release proceeded, which is the standard we would expect of any vendor and so apply to ourselves.
narratives.5.10:
Insider risk is monitored with the same seriousness we apply to customer risk. The Analyst Behavior module runs user and entity behaviour analytics over the firm's own compliance staff, watching for the signatures that precede misuse: bulk exports, off-hours activity, unusual override rates and reconnaissance against the audit record. Its commitment is to earliness. Insider-threat alerts are surfaced before findings rather than after them, and every alert carries CSV evidence export so that what the analytics observed can be handed over intact to whoever must act on it.

The MLRO and security jointly own the module, a deliberate pairing so that no single function both operates the monitoring and is its subject. Alerts arrive as unreviewed items, are reviewed jointly, and are escalated pending decision where conduct may carry disciplinary or regulatory consequence, before the matter completes and closes. Monitoring runs continuously over a configurable window, so the lookback can be tuned to the behaviour under examination without gaps in coverage.

Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.20's internal-controls requirements and SOC2 CC7.4 are the basis, and both treat the people operating a control environment as part of its threat model. Every alert, review and disposition is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, which carries a quiet symmetry: an analyst probing the audit chain is recorded by the very chain being probed. The module's board attests daily at 06:00 UTC. Records are retained for five years and archived rather than deleted, preserving the behavioural record even after staff depart, so questions about a former employee's conduct can be answered from evidence rather than recollection.
narratives.5.11:
The Board receives its view of the compliance programme through this module, and the view is built from the controls themselves. The Board Dashboard presents programme posture for senior management oversight, with every board-pack metric generated from live controls rather than assembled by hand; nothing on the dashboard is a narrative claim that cannot be traced to an operating control beneath it. Review takes place at governance committee cadence, so what the committee sees is the programme as it stands, not as it stood when slides were last prepared.

Senior-management accountability under FDL 10/2025 Art.20 and CBUAE AML Standards §6 is the basis, and the module is built around the logic of those provisions: management cannot be accountable for a programme it sees only through intermediated summaries. The MLRO owns the dashboard and carries it to the Board, and that reporting line is itself the escalation route. Matters the dashboard surfaces enter as unreviewed arrivals, pass through review, and are escalated pending decision where they require governance judgement before closure.

Every generated pack, metric snapshot and committee review is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, the module's board carries its daily 06:00 UTC attestation, and records are retained for ten years on an archive-never-delete basis, a horizon matching the governance record itself. The consequence for inspection is direct. What the Board was shown, and when, is provable; what it decided on that information sits in the same chain; and the distance between the firm's controls and its governance is, by construction, a single generation step rather than a chain of retellings. Accountability at the top rests on evidence generated at the bottom.
narratives.5.12:
Key risk indicators are tracked here continuously against the firm's risk-appetite bands, and breaches escalate. That sentence is nearly the whole control, and its brevity is the point: appetite is only meaningful if someone is measuring distance to it at all times, and escalation is only credible if it is automatic rather than discretionary. The KRI Dashboard gives the MLRO and the Board a standing view of each indicator's position within its band, trending towards or away from tolerance, with breaches lifted out of the noise the moment they occur. FATF R.1 anchors the module, since the risk-based approach assumes not only that risks are assessed but that the firm knows, day by day, whether it is operating inside the appetite its governance has set.

Ownership reflects that division of labour: the MLRO operates the dashboard and escalates, while the Board owns the appetite the indicators are measured against, and a breach therefore travels upward by design rather than by exception. Breach items enter the queue as unreviewed arrivals, are taken into review, and are escalated pending decision where a breach requires response or a band requires resetting, closing only on a recorded outcome. Every indicator reading that evidences a breach, every escalation and every band decision is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board carries a daily attestation at 06:00 UTC. Records are retained for five years, archived at the end of the period and never deleted. An inspector can therefore replay the firm's relationship with its own risk appetite across the whole retention window: where the indicators stood, when they crossed, who was told and what was done, in that order and on the record.
narratives.5.13:
Who may do what on this platform is decided in this module and remembered permanently. Access Control administers users, roles and module-level permissions, with role-based access control enforced across the estate and an immutable audit trail beneath every permission change. The pairing matters: enforcement without an immutable record proves nothing in hindsight, and a record without enforcement proves only intent. Here both hold, and a permission change cannot occur unlogged.

The Administrator owns day-to-day administration with the MLRO as joint owner and escalation point, an arrangement that keeps segregation of duties real: the person granting access is not the person who answers to the supervisor for the access regime. Changes and sensitive access events enter the queue as unreviewed arrivals, pass through review, and are escalated pending decision where a grant is anomalous or privileged, before completion and closure.

The control operates per change and continuously, in the sense that every change is captured at the moment it is made and the enforcement layer never sleeps between changes. Logical access control and segregation of duties under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.20, SOC2 CC6.1 and Cabinet 10/2019 Art.21 form the basis, three provisions that converge on a single demand: access must be deliberate, role-bound and provable.

Every grant, revocation, role change and permission decision is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module's board posts its attestation daily at 06:00 UTC. Records are retained for ten years and archived, never deleted, outliving the accounts they describe. When an inspector asks who could approve a filing in a given month, or who held administrator rights on a given day, the answer is produced from the chain rather than reconstructed from memory.
narratives.8.01:
Sanctions screening is the most frequently exercised control on the platform. The Screening module checks names and entities against the UNSC, OFAC and EU CFSP lists and the local lists, scoring each potential match and disambiguating common names from true ones so that reviewer attention lands where it belongs. Screening runs at onboarding, on transactions and on list updates, which means a change to a sanctions list propagates across the existing book rather than waiting for customers to present themselves again.

Mandatory sanctions, PEP and adverse-media screening is grounded in Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025, Cabinet 74/2020 and FATF R.6, and the module's routing reflects the seriousness of that footing. The Compliance Officer owns day-to-day operation, but a hit is never cleared at first line: every hit routes to the MLRO. On the board, items enter as new screens, pass into under review for disambiguation and scoring, and either clear or stand as hits escalated to the MLRO until a ruling is recorded.

What separates screening evidence from screening assertion is the chain. Every screen, score, disambiguation step and clearance is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, the module attests on its board daily at 06:00 UTC, and results are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis. An inspector can therefore select any name on any date and see which lists it was screened against, what the score was, and who cleared or escalated it. Negative results are kept with the same care as hits, because proving the absence of a match is half of what screening exists to do.
narratives.8.02:
Transactions are watched as they happen, not reconstructed afterwards. The Transaction Monitor applies behavioural monitoring across the firm's activity, with DPMS-specific threshold rules and typology flagging tuned to the patterns that matter in the precious metals and stones trade. It runs continuously, per transaction. There is no batch window in which activity passes unexamined.

Critical alerts do not wait for a human to notice them. When an alert crosses the critical line, the module auto-opens a case, so investigation begins as a system consequence rather than a discretionary act. Below that line, threshold and typology rules feed alerts into a worked queue owned by the Compliance Officer, with escalation to the MLRO where review hardens suspicion rather than dissolving it.

The board's progression mirrors the statutory pathway. An alert enters as new, moves to under review, and where the facts demand it is escalated to the MLRO; from there the path runs either to a suspicious report being filed or to clearance where the explanation holds. Each stage transition, each rule that fired and each disposition is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module attests daily at 06:00 UTC on its board. Alert records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted, and that includes the alerts that cleared, because the decision not to escalate is a decision the firm must be able to defend.

The obligation served is ongoing transaction monitoring under MOE Circular 08/AML/2021, Cabinet 10/2019 Art.7 and FATF R.20. The module is the firm's standing answer to all three, and it generates its evidence in the same motion as its decisions.
narratives.8.03:
Once a relationship is on the books, the duty shifts from knowing the customer to keeping that knowledge alive. Cabinet 10/2019 Art.7, Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.12 and FATF R.10 frame this as ongoing, perpetual monitoring, and the Ongoing Monitor discharges it by schedule. High-risk subjects are re-screened twice daily, medium-risk daily and low-risk weekly, and an AI pattern scan runs alongside the re-screening, writing its observations to the case timeline so that screening output and behavioural signal sit on a single record. The cadence is enforced by the scheduler rather than by diary discipline; a high-risk subject cannot drift to weekly attention because attention drifted.

The Compliance Officer owns the schedule and its exceptions. The board reflects an executor rather than a queue of judgements runs appear as scheduled, move to running, and any failure parks at the failed stage pending retry instead of vanishing. A failed run is itself a recorded event, and the retry path exists so that no monitoring window passes unexamined without trace. Every run, result and timeline entry is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module attests daily at 06:00 UTC on its board. Results are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete footing. In inspection terms the module makes a precise claim and then proves it: that each subject was re-screened at the frequency its risk tier required, on the dates that tier implies, with the outcome of every pass preserved in order, both on the chain and on the case timeline it feeds.
narratives.8.04:
Investigations need a spine, and the Cases module provides it. Whatever raises a concern - a screening hit, a monitoring alert, a referral from another module - lands here as a case, and from that moment the matter has a number, an owner, a timeline and an obligation to end in a disposition. The rule admits no quiet endings. Every case carries a recorded disposition, and every disposition links to its evidence in the audit chain, so conclusion and basis travel together and neither can be produced without the other.

The duties at work are the investigation and record-keeping obligations of FDL 10/2025, Cabinet 10/2019 Art.24 and FATF R.10/R.20. The Compliance Officer owns investigations at first instance and the MLRO takes escalations, and the module operates to the control cadence each class of case attracts rather than to a single timetable. On the board a matter arrives unreviewed, moves into review as the investigation is worked, and where decision authority is needed sits at escalated pending decision until the MLRO rules; completion and closure follow the disposition.

The case-level audit trail is the module's particular contribution to inspection readiness. Each note, document, escalation and disposition is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module attests on its board daily at 06:00 UTC. Case records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted. The effect is that any investigation the firm has conducted can be re-read end to end: what prompted it, what was found, who decided, and on what evidence. A case that cannot answer those four questions does not close.
narratives.8.05:
Targeted financial sanctions admit no risk-based discretion, and this module is built accordingly. It administers the firm's UAE TFS obligations end to end: registration with the Executive Office, NAS/ARS handling, and screening against the local control list and UN designations. The MLRO owns the module and the freeze decision personally.

Screening runs on every customer and every transaction, and re-runs whenever the lists update. On a confirmed match the control is absolute: assets are frozen without delay, inside a 24-hour SLA, before any commercial consideration is entertained. For a precious-metals dealer, where a single consignment can carry designation-relevant value, that speed is the substance of the control rather than an aspiration.

Cabinet 74/2020, the UNSC List and EOCN guidance form the legal basis, and the workflow is engineered to their tempo. Items enter as new declarations and are examined; dual-use concerns are flagged for focused review, and clearance is a recorded decision rather than a default state. An unresolved item cannot drift to cleared.

Every screen, match adjudication, freeze and clearance writes to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, with the module board attesting daily at 06:00 UTC; records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted. The record exists so the firm can demonstrate to the Executive Office and the Ministry of Economy not merely that it froze when required, but how quickly, on what match evidence, and under whose authority. In an inspection, the freeze timeline is reconstructed from the chain itself rather than from recollection.
narratives.8.06:
When the Executive Office circulates a new designation, the clock starts. This module monitors the EOCN alert channel continuously, parses incoming designation emails and automatically raises a tracked Asana task the moment a new listing appears, together with a trigger to re-screen the affected population. The control standard is action within 24 hours of designation, and ownership sits jointly with the MLRO and the Compliance Officer. For a precious-metals dealer the most exposed population is the counterparty book, and the re-screen trigger covers it in full.

Cabinet 74/2020 requires continuous monitoring of the local terrorist list and the UN lists, and the design follows that requirement literally: monitoring is continuous, not scheduled, so the firm's exposure window is measured from the designation itself rather than from the next batch run. Each alert lands on the board unreviewed, is examined in review, and any unresolved match question escalates pending decision before the task can complete and close. The auto-created task is the tracking instrument; no designation is actioned informally or off-board. Where a re-screen surfaces a potential match, the item passes to the name-match adjudication queue rather than being resolved inside this module.

Every alert received, task raised, re-screen triggered and disposition recorded is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24. The module board carries the daily 06:00 UTC attestation, and records are retained for five years on an archive-never-delete basis. The evidential yield is a timeline an inspector can read directly: designation received, task created, re-screen run, outcome recorded, each step timestamped against the 24-hour standard the firm holds itself to.
narratives.8.07:
A positive match against the UAE or UNSC lists is the most consequential event the screening estate can produce, and this module gives it a dedicated reporting workflow. Confirmed positive matches generate a Positive Name Match Report to the Executive Office, with freeze-without-delay handling applied in parallel so the report never outruns the freeze, nor the freeze the report. The MLRO owns the queue and stands behind every report submitted.

Cabinet 74/2020, the EOCN Executive Office guidance and FATF R.6 frame the obligation: designated parties must be frozen and the authorities informed of the match through the prescribed report. Queue items arrive unreviewed, are worked in review, and matters of doubt are escalated and held pending decision before completion; closed reports remain on the board's archive. The module operates on its assigned control cadence, which in practice means it is driven entirely by match events. Weeks may pass without a PNMR; when one is required, nothing about it may arrive late.

Retention here is deliberately longer than the estate's default. PNMR records are kept for ten years, archived and never deleted, reflecting the gravity of a confirmed designation event and the long tail of enquiries it can generate. Every report drafted, freeze applied and submission made is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain maintained under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module board carries the daily 06:00 UTC integrity attestation. For a DPMS firm the stakes are physical as well as financial, since a frozen counterparty may hold metal in transit; the module's record shows the freeze and the report landing together, which is precisely what the Executive Office expects to see.
narratives.8.08:
No potential name match is left to judgement-by-silence. The CNMR module operates the firm's confirmed and potential name-match queue: every potential match generated by screening is adjudicated to a recorded outcome, and every confirmed match is notified to the FIU. The MLRO owns the queue, the adjudications and the notifications, and the operating tempo is on each alert, without delay.

In the DPMS sector, where counterparties and beneficial owners change with the trade, the queue is rarely empty and never unowned. Adjudication standards are uniform whether the alert arises at onboarding, in ongoing screening or from a list-update re-screen.

An alert enters the queue unreviewed and moves into review, where the match is examined against identifiers, date-of-birth data and contextual evidence. A match the reviewer cannot eliminate escalates pending decision to the MLRO; a confirmed match proceeds to FIU notification before the item is completed and closed. False positives are dispositioned with reasons, because the discipline lies in evidencing the elimination as carefully as the confirmation.

Cabinet 74/2020 and Cabinet 10/2019 supply the obligation: matches must be adjudicated and the FIU notified, and the inspection question is whether the firm can prove both, at speed, for every alert. Each adjudication, escalation and notification writes to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, the board posts its daily attestation at 06:00 UTC, and records are retained for five years, archived, never deleted. Read alongside the sanctions-alert and PNMR modules, the queue gives the Ministry of Economy a complete picture of how a name travels from screening hit to final disposition.
narratives.8.09:
Suspicious transaction reports leave the firm through one door, and the MLRO holds the key. The STR/SAR Filing Suite applies a four-eyes quality review to every STR before it is filed, verifying the accuracy of the report's content under a dual-control QA gate, and only then generating the GOAML XML export for submission. The MLRO is the sole filing authority. Nothing reaches the FIU that the MLRO has not personally reviewed and released, and nothing the MLRO releases has escaped a second pair of eyes on the way.

Timing follows the statute rather than the workload. Reports are handled per report and filed without delay once suspicion is formed. The obligations engaged are STR reporting to the FIU under Cabinet 10/2019 Art.17-18, Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 and FATF R.20, and the module's design accepts the premise of all three: a late or inaccurate report is a failed report. On the board, a matter takes shape in draft, passes into MLRO review, and on release is marked filed to GOAML before closing.

The evidential treatment is strict because the subject matter demands it. Every draft, QA verification, release decision and export is written to the append-only, hash-linked audit chain under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 Art.24, and the module attests daily at 06:00 UTC on its board. Filing records are retained for five years, archived and never deleted. The chain therefore shows, for each STR, the interval between suspicion and filing, the quality review it received, and the MLRO's release, which is precisely the sequence an inspector or the FIU would wish to test, and the firm prefers that the answer already exists before the question is asked.
